comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses the problem of incorporating qualitative causal prior knowledge of the form “X is (is not) a causal ancestor of Y” into Markov equivalence classes of causal graphical models (PAGs and PDAGs). The authors introduce a new graphical object (PC-PAG / PC-PDAG) to represent the subset of MAGs/BNs that are both consistent with an input PAG/PDAG and the set of path-constraints. They present recursive search algorithms with forward checking and a pruning rule to identify all implied invariant edge-marks; a branch-and-bound variant handles inconsistent prior knowledge by selecting a highest-scoring consistent subset. Experiments on synthetic networks and a real biological dataset show that even a small number of path-constraints can orient a substantial fraction of previously uncertain edge-marks. Code availability is noted.

Strengths:
- The paper tackles a clear and practically relevant gap: incorporation of possibly indirect causal constraints into equivalence classes, which are semantically different from direct-edge constraints.
- Formalization of the Path-Constrained PAG/PDAG is sensible and well-motivated; the definition and examples clarify why PAGs/PDAGs are not closed under these constraints and why dashed/indirect edges are needed.
- Algorithms are well described: chronological backtracking with forward checking, a concrete pruning rule that preserves completeness, and a practical branch-and-bound treatment of inconsistent knowledge with a flexible scoring model.
- Empirical evaluation includes both synthetic experiments and a real-data case study (Sachs flow-cytometry), demonstrating non-trivial inference gains and that pruning yields large computational savings.
- The work is likely useful for practitioners who have experimental/subject-matter-derived ancestor constraints and want to tighten causal models learned from observational data.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Scalability and computational cost: for PAGs the authors impose a hard limit (e.g., 50 uncertainties) in experiments; the approach requires potentially exponential search despite pruning. More detailed runtime and scalability analysis (e.g., wall-clock times, memory, behavior on larger PAGs) would help users judge applicability to larger problems.
- Incomplete propagation for PAGs: the paper acknowledges no complete set of orientation propagation rules is known for PAGs, so it applies a subset (R1–R3) and must then validate candidate MAGs to ensure membership in the original equivalence class. This can be expensive and could produce inefficiencies; more discussion or empirical quantification of the impact of this incompleteness would strengthen the paper.
- Comparisons to prior alternative methods are limited. The paper cites an MCMC-based approach but does not empirically compare with it (or other techniques that incorporate experiment-derived constraints). A quantitative head-to-head or at least runtime/quality discussion would increase confidence in practical advantages.
- Assumptions: the method assumes the input PAG/PDAG and the provided path-constraints are correct (except when using the branch-and-bound relaxation). In practice, constraints inferred from small experiments can be noisy; while the branch-and-bound scoring addresses inconsistency, the modeling assumptions (independence of constraint probabilities, impact of correlated constraints) could be discussed further.
- No explicit theoretical complexity results are given (beyond experimental evidence); a short discussion of worst-case complexity and typical-case behavior would be useful.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Provide more extensive runtime/scalability reporting (absolute runtimes, memory) and tests on larger real-world networks where possible.
- Empirically compare against existing approaches that can incorporate indirect causal information (e.g., MCMC-based methods) to show trade-offs in accuracy and runtime.
- Investigate and report the effect of noisy/incorrect prior knowledge in more depth (robustness experiments), and possibly integrate probabilistic handling of constraints more tightly rather than the current scoring heuristic that assumes independence.
- Explore stronger propagation rules for PAGs or heuristics that reduce reliance on expensive validity checks; alternatively, characterize when the current propagation suffices.
- Include clearer pointers or proofs for claimed soundness/completeness results (if not already in supplemental material).

Overall assessment and recommendation:
This paper makes a meaningful and original contribution to causal discovery by formalizing how to integrate path (ancestor) constraints into equivalence classes and by providing practical algorithms with promising empirical results. Some limitations remain in scalability and in the incompleteness of PAG propagation, and more empirical comparison with alternatives would strengthen the work. Nevertheless, the contributions are of clear interest to the causal inference and machine learning communities and likely to be useful in practice. I therefore recommend acceptance and dissemination; the paper is suitable for an oral presentation.
